title: 'WPS is ours… no ifs and buts'
sub_topics: 2016 Arbitral Award (Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China)
Article XIII Section 2 — marine wealth reserved for Filipino citizens
traditional fishing rights in archipelagic waters
constitutional limits on executive foreign policy

stances.claim: Duterte's SONA assertion that 'the WPS is ours, no ifs and buts' is a legal and rhetorical victory — he has not waived the Arbitral Award or maritime rights, only deferred aggressive enforcement.
stances.rhetorical_move: rhetorical reframing of presidential ambivalence as preserved option

stances.claim: While Carpio is correct on the textual reading of Article XIII Section 2, a liberal interpretation allows the President to enter into a treaty regarding EEZ resource use — provided two-thirds of the Senate concurs.
stances.rhetorical_move: soft disagreement with Carpio + constitutional liberal-vs-textual contrast

stances.claim: Critics need not fear a lopsided Duterte-Xi agreement because any such treaty would be invalid without Senate concurrence — the constitutional safeguard is structural, not dependent on the President's good faith.

Under Philippine law, the President determines foreign policy subject only to constitutional and statutory limits — critics' rallies and rants 'amount to nothing' unless the President accepts them; that is his prerogative.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal assertion of executive primacy in foreign affairs

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)

signature_phrases: WPS is ours… no ifs and buts
a time to negotiate and a time to quarrel
register_markers: The learned justice may be correct… However, I think
decision_framework_signals: applies textual-vs-liberal constitutional interpretation dichotomy to resolve EEZ resource-use question
frames structural safeguards (Senate concurrence) as superior protection to political constraint on the President
frames executive foreign-policy primacy as institutional baseline absent constitutional violation
